title: CPU tier RUM export schema for the tier distribution simulator

provenance.source: An anonymized export in the same aggregate shape a site would produce from first-party cpu_tier beacons. It is checked in so every preset used by the demo is inspectable and citeable.
provenance.query: SELECT audience_segment, cpu_tier, COUNT(*) AS visits FROM app.rum_cpu_tier_beacons WHERE event_date BETWEEN @start_date AND @end_date AND cpu_tier BETWEEN 0 AND 4 GROUP BY audience_segment, cpu_tier ORDER BY audience_segment, cpu_tier;
limitations: Chrome UX Report BigQuery documents public RUM aggregation patterns, but it does not currently publish navigator.cpuPerformance tier histograms.
Do not treat this bundled export as Chrome-wide market share. Replace it with your own first-party export before making product rollout decisions.
Rows are aggregate counts only; no raw device model, CPU model, or user identifier is retained.

citedSources.label: CPU Performance API draft
citedSources.note: Defines navigator.cpuPerformance as a secure-context unsigned short tier, with 0 for unknown and 1-4 for current tiers.

citedSources.label: Chrome UX Report BigQuery documentation
citedSources.note: Documents public RUM aggregation, monthly exports, country/device dimensions, and materialized summary tables.

citedSources.label: Device Memory API bucketing background
citedSources.note: Explains the privacy model for coarse device capability buckets.

exports.label: Sample RUM export: global app
exports.description: A balanced demo-app export for showing how audience histograms drive default bundle decisions.

exports.label: Sample RUM export: mobile-heavy app
exports.description: A lower-end demo export that keeps heavy features gated until an explicit high-quality action.

exports.label: Sample RUM export: managed fleet
exports.description: A managed-device demo export where balanced and heavy code paths cover most sessions.

exports.label: Sample RUM export: kiosk / POS
exports.description: A locked-down-device demo export where low-end tiers dominate and the heavy path should stay opt-in.
